questions.question: Why do most production compilers (GCC, Clang, Go) hand-write their lexers instead of using a generator like flex?
options: Generated lexers are always faster than hand-written ones
Hand-written lexers provide better error messages, easier handling of context-sensitive tokens, and no build-time dependency on a code generator
Lexer generators cannot produce working lexers
Hand-written lexers require fewer lines of code
questions.explanation: Production compilers hand-write lexers for better error messages, context-sensitive handling (e.g., Python indentation, C preprocessor directives), tighter performance, and independence from external code-generation tools.

questions.question: In a hand-written scanner, what is the standard technique for distinguishing keywords from identifiers?
options: Hard-code a separate branch for each keyword in the scanner's main loop
Scan the full alphanumeric sequence, then look it up in a keyword table; if found, emit a keyword token, otherwise emit an identifier token
Use a separate DFA for each keyword
Require keywords to be quoted in source code
questions.explanation: The standard approach is to scan the full identifier-like sequence first, then check it against a keyword table. This is simpler than branching on every keyword in the main dispatch and makes adding new keywords trivial.

questions.question: When scanning a number literal, what must the scanner check to distinguish between hex (0xFF), binary (0b101), and decimal (42) integers?
options: The number of digits in the literal
Whether the literal starts with '0' and what the next character is ('x', 'b', or 'o')
Whether the literal contains a decimal point
The sign of the number
questions.explanation: The scanner peeks at the character after a leading '0'. If it is 'x' or 'X', it scans hex digits. If 'b' or 'B', binary digits. If 'o' or 'O', octal digits. Otherwise, it continues as a decimal integer.

questions.question: What is the advantage of accumulating errors rather than stopping at the first error during scanning?
options: It makes the scanner faster
It allows the compiler to report multiple errors in a single pass, giving the programmer a more complete picture of what needs fixing
It eliminates the need for a parser
It removes the need for error tokens
questions.explanation: By recording errors and continuing to scan, the compiler can report all lexical errors in one compilation run. This saves the programmer from fixing one error at a time and recompiling repeatedly.

questions.question: Why might a compiler developer choose a hand-written lexer over a generated one (like flex)?
options: Generated lexers are always faster
Hand-written lexers offer better error recovery, easier keyword handling, and more control over performance
Generated lexers can't handle all languages
Hand-written lexers require less code
questions.explanation: Hand-written scanners give full control: better error messages (showing exact position), ability to handle context-sensitive tokens (like distinguishing keywords from identifiers), and often better performance due to optimized switch statements rather than DFA table lookups.
